chapter: Chapter 12: Magnetic Effects of Electric Current

questions.question: Derive an expression for the magnetic field at the centre of a current-carrying circular loop.
questions.answer: Using Biot-Savart law, for a circular loop of radius r carrying current I, the field at centre B = (μ₀ I)/(2r). All dB components add up along the axis due to symmetry.

questions.question: A straight conductor carries current 5 A. Find magnetic field at a point 10 cm away from it.
Explain why the magnetic field inside a long solenoid is uniform while outside it is almost zero.
questions.answer: Ampere's circuital law: ∮ B·dl = μ₀ I_enclosed. Inside, B is constant and parallel → B × length = μ₀ (n I length) → B = μ₀ n I. Outside, I_enclosed = 0 → B ≈ 0.

questions.question: Calculate the force on a 20 cm long wire carrying 4 A current perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T.
A current-carrying rectangular coil is placed in uniform magnetic field with its plane parallel to field. Will it experience force or torque? Explain.
questions.answer: No net force (equal and opposite forces on opposite sides cancel). Torque τ = N I A B sinθ = 0 (θ = 0° or 180°), so no torque either. Coil remains in equilibrium.

questions.question: Explain the working of a DC motor using Fleming’s left-hand rule and the role of split ring commutator.
questions.answer: Current in coil experiences force (Fleming’s left-hand rule). Forces on opposite sides produce torque → rotation. Commutator reverses current every half turn so torque direction remains same.

questions.question: Why does induced emf in a coil increase when a stronger magnet is moved faster towards it?
questions.answer: Faraday’s law: emf = – dΦ/dt. Faster motion → higher dΦ/dt. Stronger magnet → higher flux Φ → larger change in flux per unit time → higher emf.

questions.question: A coil of 200 turns and area 0.05 m² is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.4 T. If field is reduced to zero in 0.2 s, calculate induced emf.
Explain Lenz’s law with an example of a magnet approaching a coil.
questions.answer: Lenz’s law: Induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux. When north pole approaches coil, induced current creates north pole facing the magnet to repel it → opposes increase in flux.

questions.question: Why is the core of a transformer made of laminated soft iron?
questions.answer: Soft iron increases magnetic flux linkage. Lamination reduces eddy current losses by breaking large eddy paths → reduces heating and energy loss.

questions.question: In domestic circuits, why is the earth wire connected to the metal body of appliances?
questions.answer: If live wire touches metal body, current flows through earth wire (low resistance path) to ground instead of through human body → fuse melts or circuit breaker trips, preventing shock.

questions.question: A household has appliances of total power 3 kW at 220 V. Calculate the current drawn and suggest fuse rating.
questions.answer: I = P/V = 3000/220 ≈ 13.64 A. Fuse rating should be slightly higher, say 15 A or 16 A.

questions.question: Explain why overloading occurs more easily in parallel circuits than in series.
questions.answer: In parallel, each appliance draws its own current → total current = sum of individual currents. Many appliances can cause very high total current exceeding wire capacity.

questions.question: Why do we use two separate circuits (power and lighting) in household wiring?
questions.answer: Lighting circuit carries low current (thin wires), power circuit carries high current (thicker wires). Separate circuits prevent overloading of lighting wires by heavy appliances.

questions.question: A current-carrying conductor is placed between poles of a horseshoe magnet. Draw the direction of force using Fleming’s left-hand rule and explain why the conductor moves.
questions.answer: Force direction by Fleming’s left-hand rule (field from N to S, current direction → force perpendicular). Conductor moves due to Ampere’s force F = I L × B.
